title: Eligibility criteria for benefit reviews
body: A benefit review may be initiated in the following circumstances: (1) the claimant reports a change of circumstances that may affect entitlement, (2) information received from a third party suggests the claimant's circumstances have changed, (3) the claim is selected as part of the annual reassessment programme, or (4) the claim has been active for more than 24 months without a review. Reviews must be completed within 90 days of the trigger event.

title: Changes of circumstances — what triggers a review
body: The following changes of circumstances must be reported by the claimant within 14 days and will trigger a review: change of address, change of employment status or working hours, change in household composition, change in income above or below the relevant threshold, or commencement or cessation of education. Failure to report a change of circumstances within 14 days may result in recovery of overpaid amounts.

title: Evidence requirements for benefit reviews
body: When a benefit review is triggered by a change of circumstances, the caseworker must obtain: (1) proof of the new address (if applicable), (2) an income statement covering the 3 months prior to the change, and (3) a signed declaration confirming the change. If any evidence is outstanding after 28 days, the caseworker should issue a reminder. If outstanding after 56 days, the case should be escalated to a team leader.

title: Benefit review decisions — sign-off requirements
body: Decisions resulting in an award increase of more than £50 per week require sign-off from a team leader before the claimant is notified. Decisions resulting in a reduction or cessation of benefit must be accompanied by a mandatory reconsideration notice. All decisions must be notified to the claimant in writing within 5 working days of the decision being made.

title: Eligibility to hold a licence
body: An applicant for a licence must: (1) be aged 18 or over, or be a registered legal entity, (2) not have had a licence revoked within the preceding 5 years, (3) not be subject to any current court order that would prohibit the activity being licensed, and (4) hold valid public liability insurance of at least £2 million for premises or event licences. Applications that do not meet these criteria must be refused at the initial assessment stage.

title: Evidence requirements for licence applications
body: All licence applications must be accompanied by: (1) proof of identity (passport or driving licence), (2) proof of address dated within the last 3 months, (3) a completed application form, and (4) the applicable fee. Premises licences additionally require a site plan and, where regulated entertainment is proposed, a noise management plan. Event licences additionally require a site plan, crowd management plan, and evidence of public liability insurance.

title: Consultation and objections process
body: All new premises and event licence applications must be subject to a 28-day public consultation. Objections must be made in writing and must state the specific licensing objective they relate to. Objections from responsible authorities (police, fire service, environmental health) carry additional weight and must be considered in full. Where two or more substantive objections are received, the application must be referred to a senior caseworker before a decision is issued.

title: Triggers for compliance checks
body: A compliance check may be initiated in the following circumstances: (1) as part of the scheduled annual inspection programme, (2) following receipt of a complaint or referral from a third party, (3) where intelligence suggests non-compliance, or (4) where a previous inspection identified issues requiring follow-up. Compliance checks triggered by third-party referrals must be acknowledged within 5 working days and commenced within 20 working days.

title: Evidence gathering in compliance checks
body: The caseworker must request relevant documentation at the outset of the compliance check. The organisation must respond within 28 days. Where a site visit is required, the caseworker must provide at least 5 working days' notice except in cases where there is a risk that evidence may be concealed or destroyed. Site visits must be conducted by at least two officers. A written record of all findings must be completed within 3 working days of the visit.

title: Enforcement thresholds and escalation
body: Where a compliance check identifies a minor breach (defined as a breach that does not present a risk to the public or to individuals), the caseworker may issue a written warning and allow 28 days for remediation. Where a serious breach is identified (defined as a breach that presents a risk to the public, involves financial irregularity, or involves a repeated failure), the case must be escalated to a senior officer within 2 working days. Legal proceedings may be recommended by the senior officer where the breach meets the relevant threshold.
